u1-L1-Q1.0: Subtraction only works for collinear (same-line) forces, and multiplying the largest has no physical basis.
u1-L1-Q1.1: Averaging magnitudes discards direction information and does not yield the correct resultant vector.
u1-L1-Q1.3: Concurrent forces cancel only when they sum to zero; having the same point of application does not guarantee cancellation.

u1-L1-Q2.0: Angles become unmanageable in 3D; component resolution is universally preferred over working with spatial angles directly.
u1-L1-Q2.2: Component resolution is needed regardless of force magnitude; it is a mathematical technique, not a threshold-based rule.
u1-L1-Q2.3: The parallelogram law works for two forces at a time and becomes cumbersome for many forces in 3D.

u1-L1-Q3.0: When net force is zero but net moment is nonzero, the system reduces to a couple, not a single force.

u1-L1-Q4.0: Direction cosines can be negative (for obtuse angles), and they must satisfy the unit vector constraint, not just lie in [0,1].
u1-L1-Q4.1: Direction cosines can be negative when the angle with an axis exceeds 90 degrees.
u1-L1-Q4.2: The constraint is on the sum of squares equaling 1, not the arithmetic sum of the cosines.

u1-L1-Q5.0: Using the full magnitude times horizontal distance ignores the angle and overstates the moment.
u1-L1-Q5.1: Scale drawings are imprecise and impractical for engineering calculations requiring accuracy.
u1-L1-Q5.3: Rotating coordinates adds complexity; Varignon's theorem resolves components with the existing axes.

u1-L1-Q7.1: 200 N assumes simple addition of magnitudes, which ignores the 60-degree angle between the forces.
u1-L1-Q7.2: 141.4 N corresponds to cos(45) scaling, which applies only when forces are at 90 degrees apart.
u1-L1-Q7.3: 100 N would only result if the forces cancelled in one direction, which does not occur at 60 degrees.

u1-L1-Q8.0: 354 N for both components implies a 45-degree angle (cos45 = sin45), not 30 degrees.
u1-L1-Q8.1: This swaps sine and cosine: Fx uses cosine (larger component) and Fy uses sine for a 30-degree angle.
u1-L1-Q8.3: Fy = 0 would mean the force is purely horizontal, contradicting the stated 30-degree elevation.

u1-L1-Q9.0: Collinear equal-and-opposite forces produce zero net force and zero net moment, which is full cancellation, not a couple.

u1-L1-Q10.0: Equilibrium uniquely fixes both magnitude and direction of the third force, not just magnitude.
u1-L1-Q10.2: The direction is fully determined by -(F1+F2); it does not depend on the coordinate system used.
u1-L1-Q10.3: The third force is uniquely determined as -(F1+F2); there is exactly one solution.

u1-L1-Q11.0: 53.1 degrees is the reference angle arctan(30/40) in Q1, but the force lies in Q2 since Fx is negative.
u1-L1-Q11.1: 36.9 degrees is arctan(30/40) measured from the wrong reference axis.
u1-L1-Q11.3: 216.9 degrees places the force in Q3, but Fy is positive so the force must be in Q2.

u1-L1-Q12.0: M = Fd requires finding perpendicular distance d, which is often impractical in 3D geometry.
u1-L1-Q12.1: The cross product works for any orientation, not just when force and position are perpendicular.
u1-L1-Q12.2: The cross product is a 3D tool; the number of concurrent forces in 2D is irrelevant.

u1-L1-Q13.0: The moment vector M = r x F is a physical quantity whose magnitude and direction are independent of coordinate choice.

u1-L1-Q14.0: (3, 4, 0) is the displacement vector, not the unit vector; it has magnitude 5, not 1.
u1-L1-Q14.2: (0.5, 0.5, 0) does not correspond to the 3:4 ratio of the displacement components.
u1-L1-Q14.3: (0.707, 0.707, 0) corresponds to a 45-degree direction, inconsistent with the 3:4 component ratio.

u1-L1-Q15.1: The dot product yields a scalar, not a vector, so it cannot give a resultant force magnitude.
u1-L1-Q15.2: Moments require the cross product (r x F), not the dot product.
u1-L1-Q15.3: The dot product measures alignment between vectors and is fundamental to projection calculations in statics.

u1-L1-Q17.0: Net force is +10 - 20 + 10 = 0 N, so there is no resultant force at all, let alone 40 N.
u1-L1-Q17.1: Net force is zero, so no single equivalent force exists.
u1-L1-Q17.2: Net moment about x = 0 is also zero (0 - 60 + 60 = 0), so there is no residual couple.

u1-L1-Q18.0: Six equations apply to 3D rigid bodies; a 2D body has only 3 degrees of freedom.
u1-L1-Q18.1: Two equations omit rotational equilibrium, which is essential for a rigid body that can rotate.
u1-L1-Q18.3: Four equations would overdetermine a 2D system with only 3 independent equilibrium conditions.

u1-L1-Q19.1: For three non-parallel, non-concurrent coplanar forces in equilibrium, their lines of action must be concurrent.

u1-L1-Q20.0: 190 N is the arithmetic sum of components, not the vector magnitude using the Pythagorean theorem.
u1-L1-Q20.2: 70 N is the sum of only two components, omitting the 120k component entirely.
u1-L1-Q20.3: 110 N does not match the correct calculation: sqrt(900 + 1600 + 14400) = sqrt(16900) = 130.

u1-L1-Q21.1: This describes a literal hand tool, not the force-system reduction concept from statics.
u1-L1-Q21.2: A wrench includes a force component and is not limited to shear loading without bending.
u1-L1-Q21.3: A couple is a special case (zero net force); a wrench specifically includes a nonzero force.

u1-L1-Q22.0: 692.8 N is only the x-component from cable A alone, ignoring cable B's negative x-contribution.
u1-L1-Q22.1: 1100 N incorrectly adds the magnitudes directly without accounting for their different directions.
u1-L1-Q22.3: 200 N does not correspond to the correct vector addition of the two cable force components.

u1-L1-Q23.1: By the triangle inequality, |F1+F2| is always less than or equal to |F1|+|F2|, with equality only when parallel.

u1-L1-Q24.0: 1000 N would only apply if the component direction aligned exactly with the original force.
u1-L1-Q24.1: 938 N does not correspond to the correct sine rule calculation for the given angles.
u1-L1-Q24.3: 500 N would result only from a symmetric 60-60-60 degree triangle, which does not match 40 and 65 degrees.

u1-L1-Q25.0: 100 N-m ignores the sin30 factor needed for the perpendicular distance from pivot to force line.
u1-L1-Q25.1: 86.6 N-m uses cos30 instead of sin30 for the moment arm calculation.
u1-L1-Q25.3: 25 N-m incorrectly uses sin30 twice or halves the already-correct result.

u1-L1-Q27.1: 350 N-m would require adding the magnitudes, but opposing moments must be subtracted.
u1-L1-Q27.2: The larger clockwise moment dominates, so the net is clockwise, not counterclockwise.
u1-L1-Q27.3: 350 N-m counterclockwise both adds the magnitudes incorrectly and reverses the net direction.

u1-L1-Q28.0: Three equations cover only translational equilibrium; 3D bodies also have three rotational DOF.
u1-L1-Q28.2: There is no ninth or compatibility equation in rigid-body equilibrium; compatibility applies to deformable bodies.
u1-L1-Q28.3: Twelve equations would overdetermine the system; a 3D rigid body has exactly six DOF.

u1-L1-Q29.0: Lami's theorem requires exactly three concurrent, coplanar forces in equilibrium, not any number of forces.

u1-L1-Q30.0: Adding an equal-and-opposite force at A cancels F but does not reproduce its effect at B.
u1-L1-Q30.1: Doubling the force at B changes the net force on the body and violates equilibrium.
u1-L1-Q30.2: Forces can only slide along their line of action on rigid bodies; moving off-line requires a compensating couple.

u1-L2-Q1.1: A fixed support always provides a moment reaction; omitting it makes moment equilibrium unsatisfiable.
u1-L2-Q1.2: The missing moment reaction corrupts both the shear and bending moment diagrams, not just one.
u1-L2-Q1.3: The beam does not appear indeterminate; it appears unsolvable because equilibrium cannot be satisfied.

u1-L2-Q2.1: A roller always reacts normal to its surface, so on an incline the reaction is perpendicular to the slope, not vertical.

u1-L2-Q3.0: The location of the nearest support is irrelevant; the resultant must act at the centroid of the load distribution.
u1-L2-Q3.1: Peak intensity and length alone do not capture the total force magnitude or the correct point of application.
u1-L2-Q3.3: Average intensity and moment at the support do not define the equivalent resultant force correctly.

u1-L2-Q4.1: An internal hinge does not create a new unknown; it adds a known condition (M = 0 at the hinge).
u1-L2-Q4.2: The hinge does not eliminate support reactions; it adds a moment condition at the hinge location.
u1-L2-Q4.3: A hinge releases rotation (allows relative rotation), it does not lock it.

u1-L2-Q5.0: 10 kN assumes the load is at midspan, but it is offset at 3 m from the left.
u1-L2-Q5.2: 7.5 kN is the right reaction, not the left; moments about the right support give the left reaction.
u1-L2-Q5.3: 20 kN would mean the left support carries the entire load, which only happens if the load is directly above it.

u1-L2-Q7.1: A pin resists both horizontal and vertical forces, not just vertical.
u1-L2-Q7.2: Three reactions describe a fixed support, not a pin; pins provide no moment resistance.
u1-L2-Q7.3: A roller provides force perpendicular to the surface; a pin provides two independent force components.

u1-L2-Q8.1: The pin provides both horizontal and vertical reactions, giving two unknowns plus the cable tension.
u1-L2-Q8.2: There is only one cable, so only one cable tension; two cable tensions would require two separate cables.
u1-L2-Q8.3: The pin must resist forces for equilibrium, so its reactions are not zero.

u1-L2-Q9.0: Internal forces between connected parts cancel in pairs on a system FBD and must not be shown as external loads.

u1-L2-Q10.0: Beam self-weight is significant in many problems and should only be ignored when explicitly stated.
u1-L2-Q10.1: The weight acts at the centroid of the uniform beam, which is the midpoint, not at the left support.
u1-L2-Q10.2: Replacing a uniform load with a single resultant at the centroid is standard practice in statics.

u1-L2-Q11.0: Total load is 10 x 6 = 60 kN; each support carries 30 kN for a symmetric loading, not 20 kN.
u1-L2-Q11.1: For symmetric loading on a simply supported beam, both reactions are equal, not concentrated on one side.
u1-L2-Q11.3: 10 kN per support sums to only 20 kN, far less than the total 60 kN load.

u1-L2-Q12.1: Shear and bending are internal force types, not a definition of a two-force member.
u1-L2-Q12.2: Having two pin joints is necessary but not sufficient; loads must act only at those two points.
u1-L2-Q12.3: A two-force member carries only axial load (tension or compression), never both simultaneously.

u1-L2-Q13.0: A smooth (frictionless) surface can only exert a normal force; tangential friction requires a rough surface.

u1-L2-Q14.1: The area under a triangular load is w0L/2, not w0L, and the centroid is at 2L/3, not L/2.
u1-L2-Q14.2: The centroid of a triangle is at 2L/3 from the light end, not at the midpoint.
u1-L2-Q14.3: The magnitude is w0L/2, not w0L/3; the area formula for a triangle is base times height divided by 2.

u1-L2-Q15.0: Having more unknowns than equations means the structure is indeterminate, not necessarily unstable.
u1-L2-Q15.1: Extra unknowns are normal for redundant supports; it is not necessarily an FBD error.
u1-L2-Q15.2: Assuming a reaction is zero is physically unjustified and changes the structural model.

u1-L2-Q17.0: Direction is strictly constrained; the third force must pass through the intersection of the other two.
u1-L2-Q17.2: No perpendicularity requirement exists; the third force line must be concurrent with the first two.
u1-L2-Q17.3: The magnitude is determined by equilibrium, but the line of action must also pass through the concurrency point.

u1-L2-Q18.0: Cables can only pull (tension), not push, so they cannot act in any direction.
u1-L2-Q18.1: A roller provides force in only one direction (normal to the surface), not in any arbitrary direction.
u1-L2-Q18.2: A fixed support provides force plus moment; the question asks about a force that can act in any direction.

u1-L2-Q19.0: When the problem says to neglect weight, omitting it from the FBD is correct and simplifies the analysis.

u1-L2-Q20.0: 20 kN does not correspond to the correct moment balance for the given asymmetric loading.
u1-L2-Q20.2: 15 kN equals one of the applied loads, not the computed reaction from moment equilibrium.
u1-L2-Q20.3: 25 kN equals the other applied load; the reaction depends on both loads and their positions.

u1-L2-Q21.0: Beams primarily resist bending and shear, not just axial loads.
u1-L2-Q21.2: Normal force can be nonzero in frames and inclined members; it should not be assumed zero.
u1-L2-Q21.3: Internal forces always exist at a cut section; they are the mechanism by which loads transfer through the member.

u1-L2-Q22.1: The tension acts along the rope on each side, not as a single averaged force at the center.
u1-L2-Q22.2: For a frictionless pulley, tension is equal on both sides; friction would be needed to create different tensions.
u1-L2-Q22.3: The rope contacts the pulley at discrete entry and exit points, represented by two tension forces.

u1-L2-Q24.1: A cable is a single-force element providing only tension along its length, not two independent components.
u1-L2-Q24.2: Cables cannot provide moment reactions; they transmit only axial tension.
u1-L2-Q24.3: A cable under tension provides a reaction force along its direction; it is not passive.

u1-L2-Q25.1: A fixed support can be modeled as providing two force components plus a moment, equivalent to a pin plus a couple.

u1-L2-Q26.0: One pin is sufficient to resist horizontal load; a second pin would overconstrain the beam.
u1-L2-Q26.1: The roller is not needed to resist horizontal loads; the pin handles the horizontal force P.
u1-L2-Q26.3: The pin handles the horizontal load entirely; roller friction is irrelevant to proper constraint.

u1-L2-Q27.0: Calculating moments before drawing the FBD risks omitting forces, leading to incorrect equilibrium equations.
u1-L2-Q27.1: Writing equations without a diagram almost guarantees missing forces or incorrect directions.
u1-L2-Q27.3: Determinacy classification is done after the FBD, since you need to know the number of reactions first.

u1-L2-Q28.1: W/2 + P/4 incorrectly uses P/4 instead of 3P/4; the person at L/4 from left produces 3P/4 at the left via moments.
u1-L2-Q28.2: W/4 + P/2 underestimates both contributions from the weight distribution and the person's position.
u1-L2-Q28.3: (W+P)/2 assumes both loads act at midspan, ignoring the person's offset position.

u1-L2-Q29.1: Three non-parallel, non-concurrent reactions provide the three independent constraints needed for a 2D rigid body.

u1-L3-Q1.0: m + r = 2j is necessary but not sufficient; you must also verify geometric stability.
u1-L3-Q1.1: m + r = 14 = 2j here, so the truss is not indeterminate; it meets the determinacy condition.
u1-L3-Q1.3: m + r = 14 = 2j, so the truss is not unstable; the necessary condition for determinacy is satisfied.

u1-L3-Q2.1: Cutting through three unknowns matches the three available equilibrium equations, making the method solvable.

u1-L3-Q3.1: Zero-force members provide stability against buckling and carry load under different loading conditions.
u1-L3-Q3.2: Building codes do not require keeping structurally unnecessary members; the reason is bracing and alternate load paths.
u1-L3-Q3.3: Self-weight of slender truss members is typically negligible and is not why zero-force members are retained.

u1-L3-Q4.0: Trusses may use more material in certain configurations; the key difference is structural behavior, not cost.
u1-L3-Q4.2: Rigid joints do not guarantee greater strength; frames carry bending which requires heavier members.
u1-L3-Q4.3: Trusses can span very large distances (bridges span hundreds of meters), far exceeding 10 m.

u1-L3-Q5.0: Method of joints requires solving every joint sequentially from the supports, which is inefficient for a single internal member.
u1-L3-Q5.1: Large trusses are routinely solved by hand using method of sections; FEA is not required.
u1-L3-Q5.3: Removing zero-force members simplifies the truss but does not directly target a specific deep member.

u1-L3-Q7.0: The zero-force member rule is a standard result that can be applied by inspection at unloaded two-member joints.
u1-L3-Q7.1: With no external load and non-collinear members, equilibrium requires both forces to be zero, not in tension.
u1-L3-Q7.2: Both members must have zero force; they cannot carry equal-and-opposite loads at an unloaded joint with only two members.

u1-L3-Q8.0: The third member is perpendicular to the collinear pair's direction, so it cannot balance any component along that direction.
u1-L3-Q8.1: The non-collinear member cannot balance a net force along the collinear direction, so it must carry zero force.
u1-L3-Q8.3: This is a standard zero-force member identification rule that does not require full truss analysis.

u1-L3-Q9.1: Starting at a joint with two or fewer unknowns ensures the two equilibrium equations can solve for all unknowns at that joint.

u1-L3-Q10.1: In a Pratt truss under gravity, diagonals slope toward the supports and carry tension, not compression.
u1-L3-Q10.2: Diagonals carry significant load in a Pratt truss; they are not unloaded members.
u1-L3-Q10.3: Under symmetric gravity loading, all diagonals in a Pratt truss are in tension, not alternating.

u1-L3-Q11.0: The purpose is analytical (eliminating unknowns from the moment equation), not visual clarity.
u1-L3-Q11.2: The section cut can be any path; the moment point choice is independent of the cut path.
u1-L3-Q11.3: While the total moment is zero about any point, choosing the intersection eliminates two unknowns simultaneously.

u1-L3-Q12.0: m + r = 9 + 4 = 13 and 2j = 12, so m + r exceeds 2j, meaning it is not determinate.
u1-L3-Q12.2: m + r = 13 > 12 = 2j, so the truss has more unknowns than equations and is not unstable.
u1-L3-Q12.3: The degree of indeterminacy is m + r - 2j = 13 - 12 = 1, not 2.

u1-L3-Q13.1: Frictionless pin connections ensure only axial forces act in truss members, validating the two-force member assumption.

u1-L3-Q14.1: Both frames and trusses can be 2D or 3D; dimensionality does not distinguish them.
u1-L3-Q14.2: Joint type (welded vs. pinned) is a construction detail; the defining difference is multi-force vs. two-force members.
u1-L3-Q14.3: Frames are not inherently stronger; trusses can be more efficient for axial-load-dominated structures.

u1-L3-Q15.0: Individual frame members are analyzable; the issue is that internal forces between members are hidden in the whole-body FBD.
u1-L3-Q15.1: Frames can be statically determinate; dismembering is needed to expose internal pin forces, not because of indeterminacy.
u1-L3-Q15.3: Converting distributed loads is a separate step unrelated to why frame members must be analyzed individually.

u1-L3-Q17.0: Warren trusses can still have zero-force members depending on loading; the advantage is fewer total members.
u1-L3-Q17.1: No single truss type has the highest load capacity for all configurations; efficiency depends on span, loading, and proportions.
u1-L3-Q17.3: Lateral load resistance depends on bracing, not on whether the truss has vertical members.

u1-L3-Q18.0: Adding fictitious members changes the structural model and gives incorrect results.
u1-L3-Q18.1: This is solvable by hand using a combination of methods of joints and sections.
u1-L3-Q18.3: Assuming members are zero-force without proof gives incorrect results.

u1-L3-Q19.1: A negative result when tension was assumed means the member is actually in compression, which is the standard sign convention.

u1-L3-Q20.0: Single-member joints are not locally indeterminate; equilibrium directly forces the single member to carry zero force.
u1-L3-Q20.1: A single-member joint with no external load means the member carries no force, indicating a likely modeling error.
u1-L3-Q20.2: Reactions act at supports, not at arbitrary internal joints; a single-member joint does not carry support reactions.

u1-L3-Q21.1: Forces at three points (A, B, C) means more than two points, so it cannot be a two-force member.
u1-L3-Q21.2: Pin B transfers forces but does not make the member zero-force; forces exist at all three points.
u1-L3-Q21.3: Multi-force members are standard in frame analysis and are fully analyzable by statics.

u1-L3-Q22.0: The formula m + r vs. 2j is the standard determinacy check for any planar truss.
u1-L3-Q22.1: m + r = 5 + 3 = 8 = 2(4) = 8, so m + r equals 2j exactly, indicating determinacy rather than indeterminacy.
u1-L3-Q22.2: m + r = 8 = 2j, so the truss has sufficient members and is not an unstable mechanism.

u1-L3-Q24.0: K-trusses still have diagonal members; the K-pattern subdivides the panel with an intermediate joint.
u1-L3-Q24.2: K-trusses use more members than simpler configurations; the advantage is reduced buckling length, not less material.
u1-L3-Q24.3: All trusses require external supports; no truss configuration is self-supporting against external loads.

u1-L3-Q25.1: Solving support reactions first provides known forces at support joints, enabling sequential joint analysis.

u1-L3-Q26.0: Members carry different forces depending on position; they are not all equal.
u1-L3-Q26.1: Howe diagonals slope opposite to Pratt diagonals and carry compression under gravity loading.
u1-L3-Q26.2: Diagonals carry significant forces in a Howe truss; they are not bracing-only members.

u1-L3-Q27.1: 50 MPa would result from dividing 50 kN by 1000 mm^2, which uses the wrong area.
u1-L3-Q27.2: 25 MPa uses an area twice as large as the given 500 mm^2.
u1-L3-Q27.3: 250 MPa would require dividing by only 200 mm^2, which is not the given cross-sectional area.

u1-L3-Q28.1: Members can have different lengths; equal length is not a requirement for the two-force member assumption.
u1-L3-Q28.2: Rigid (welded) joints cause bending in members, violating the two-force member assumption.
u1-L3-Q28.3: Material and section properties affect strength, not the two-force member classification.

u1-L3-Q29.1: Machines contain moving parts and transmit/modify forces, distinguishing them from stationary frames.

u1-L4-Q1.1: The sliding angle depends on the friction coefficient, not a fixed 45-degree threshold.
u1-L4-Q1.2: Kinetic friction governs after sliding begins; static friction determines the onset of motion.
u1-L4-Q1.3: Mass cancels in the comparison; any block slides when the incline angle exceeds arctan(mu_s).

u1-L4-Q2.1: Self-locking occurs when the lead angle is less than the friction angle, preventing back-driving.

u1-L4-Q3.0: The friction coefficient is a material property that does not change with the number of wraps.
u1-L4-Q3.2: Normal force grows exponentially with contact angle, not linearly with each wrap.
u1-L4-Q3.3: Strain energy is not the mechanism; the capstan effect arises from exponentially compounding friction.

u1-L4-Q4.0: Wedges follow Newton's third law; separate FBDs are needed to expose the contact forces between surfaces.
u1-L4-Q4.2: Friction acts on all contacting surfaces, not just the wedge surfaces.
u1-L4-Q4.3: Wedge angles are geometric properties that do not change under load in rigid-body analysis.

u1-L4-Q5.0: Drop testing measures impact behavior, not the static friction coefficient.
u1-L4-Q5.1: Pushing at constant speed measures kinetic friction; the angle method captures static friction at the onset of motion.
u1-L4-Q5.3: The tilt-angle method provides a simple, equipment-free way to measure the static friction coefficient.

u1-L4-Q7.0: 49.1 N uses mu_s = 0.5, not the given 0.4.
u1-L4-Q7.1: 40 N omits the gravitational acceleration factor (g = 9.81 m/s^2) from the normal force calculation.
u1-L4-Q7.2: 98.1 N is the full weight; the friction force is only mu_s times the normal force.

u1-L4-Q8.1: Static friction is not constant; it increases with the applied force up to the maximum value mu_s * N.
u1-L4-Q8.2: Friction has an upper limit of mu_s * N; it cannot increase indefinitely.
u1-L4-Q8.3: Kinetic friction acts only after sliding begins, not from the start of force application.

u1-L4-Q9.0: Friction opposes relative motion or impending motion, not necessarily the applied force direction.

u1-L4-Q10.0: Self-locking is a geometric and friction property that can be checked without knowing the applied load.
u1-L4-Q10.1: The friction angle arctan(0.15) is about 8.53 degrees, which exceeds the 5-degree wedge angle.
u1-L4-Q10.3: The self-locking principle applies to wedges, screws, and any inclined-plane mechanism.

u1-L4-Q11.0: Tipping and sliding are independent failure modes that depend on different geometric and friction parameters.
u1-L4-Q11.1: Whether a block tips or slides first depends on its width-to-height ratio and friction coefficient.
u1-L4-Q11.2: Tipping can occur with any friction coefficient if the block is tall and narrow enough.

u1-L4-Q12.0: Coulomb friction depends only on normal force and friction coefficient, not on contact area.
u1-L4-Q12.2: Halving friction by spreading load contradicts the Coulomb model which is area-independent.
u1-L4-Q12.3: Friction does not scale with area squared; Coulomb friction is explicitly independent of contact area.

u1-L4-Q13.0: The friction coefficient is a property of the surface pair, independent of the normal force magnitude.

u1-L4-Q14.0: 170 N does not match T2 = 500/e^(0.3 * pi), which gives approximately 206 N.
u1-L4-Q14.1: 250 N assumes the tension ratio is 2:1, but the capstan equation gives an exponential ratio.
u1-L4-Q14.3: 100 N underestimates the slack-side tension; the exponential ratio for these parameters is about 2.57.

u1-L4-Q15.1: 25 N-m significantly underestimates the torque required to overcome friction and raise the load.
u1-L4-Q15.2: 100 N-m overestimates the torque for the given screw parameters.
u1-L4-Q15.3: 37.5 N-m does not match the calculation using the lead angle and friction angle for these dimensions.

u1-L4-Q17.0: Friction direction depends on the tendency of motion, which changes as the horizontal force increases.
u1-L4-Q17.1: At high P, the block tends to slide up the incline, so friction reverses to point downhill.
u1-L4-Q17.3: Friction is always tangential to the surface, never perpendicular.

u1-L4-Q18.0: 1/sin(alpha) does not account for both sides of the wedge; the correct factor involves tangent.
u1-L4-Q18.1: Steeper wedges have less mechanical advantage; a smaller angle provides greater force multiplication.
u1-L4-Q18.2: The factor of 2 accounts for both surfaces, but the relationship involves 1/tan, not tan.

u1-L4-Q19.0: Increasing weight increases the normal force and thus the friction force, but the friction coefficient itself remains unchanged.

u1-L4-Q20.1: The critical angle depends on the friction coefficient, not a fixed 45-degree value.
u1-L4-Q20.2: A smooth wall provides no friction, so all friction comes from the floor; the ladder can still slip.
u1-L4-Q20.3: This inequality is reversed; the ladder slips when the angle is too shallow, not when tan(theta) is small.

u1-L4-Q21.0: The torque integrand does depend on radius, producing the r-cubed relationship through the area and moment arm.
u1-L4-Q21.1: Friction per unit area is constant in the uniform pressure model; it is the area element and moment arm that produce r^3.
u1-L4-Q21.2: The pressure distribution can be uniform or vary inversely with r; neither follows a cubic polynomial.

u1-L4-Q22.0: This accounts for only the interface friction and ignores friction between block B and the ground.
u1-L4-Q22.1: This accounts for only the ground friction and ignores the interface friction between blocks A and B.
u1-L4-Q22.3: Adding the friction coefficients is incorrect; each coefficient multiplies its own normal force.

u1-L4-Q24.0: The increase is not simply doubled; the wedging action provides a multiplicative factor of 1/sin(beta).
u1-L4-Q24.1: The groove angle significantly increases the effective friction through the wedging action.
u1-L4-Q24.2: The wedging action increases the normal force, which increases friction rather than decreasing it.

u1-L4-Q25.0: A 10:1 mechanical advantage would yield ~500 N, but friction on multiple surfaces significantly increases the required force.
u1-L4-Q25.2: 5000 N implies no mechanical advantage, but the wedge does provide some advantage even with friction.
u1-L4-Q25.3: 875 N accounts for only one of the three friction surfaces involved in the wedge problem.

u1-L4-Q26.1: In the Coulomb model, kinetic friction depends on normal force and coefficient only, not sliding speed.

u1-L4-Q27.0: 1786 N uses an incorrect effective radius or omits a factor in the disc friction torque formula.
u1-L4-Q27.1: 7143 N overestimates the required force by using an incorrect moment arm.
u1-L4-Q27.3: 5000 N does not match the correct calculation using the disc clutch torque equation.

u1-L4-Q28.0: The proportion of energy lost to heat varies with the lead and friction angles, not a fixed 50%.
u1-L4-Q28.2: The less-than-50% efficiency is a mathematical consequence of the angles, not a deliberate design choice.
u1-L4-Q28.3: Adding lubrication reduces friction but cannot make a self-locking screw exceed 50% efficiency without losing self-locking.

u1-L4-Q29.0: Friction forces on different surfaces of a wedge point in opposite directions because they oppose relative motion on each surface.

u1-L5-Q1.0: Centroid location affects the neutral axis position, which directly impacts bending stress, not just shear.
u1-L5-Q1.1: Centroid location is critical for calculating moment of inertia and bending stress in structural design.
u1-L5-Q1.3: Centroid location matters for all cross-section shapes, not just circular ones.

u1-L5-Q2.0: The result is not slightly too high; it significantly underestimates I by missing the Ad^2 parallel axis transfer terms.
u1-L5-Q2.2: The parallel axis theorem is needed for all composite sections where components are offset from the composite centroid.
u1-L5-Q2.3: The parallel axis theorem is essential whenever individual centroids differ from the composite centroid.

u1-L5-Q3.0: The parallel axis theorem is needed only when the hole's centroid does not coincide with the plate's centroid.

u1-L5-Q4.0: A solid square wastes material near the neutral axis where stress is low.
u1-L5-Q4.1: A solid circle concentrates too much material near the neutral axis, giving a poor I-to-area ratio.
u1-L5-Q4.3: While thin-walled tubes are efficient, I-beams place material specifically at the flanges for maximum bending resistance.

u1-L5-Q5.0: 720,000 mm^4 is the I of the solid rectangle alone, without subtracting the hole.
u1-L5-Q5.2: 45,000 mm^4 is the I of the hole alone, not the net section.
u1-L5-Q5.3: 1,350,000 mm^4 incorrectly adds the hole's I instead of subtracting it.

u1-L5-Q7.0: h/2 is the centroid of a rectangle, not a triangle; the triangle's centroid is at h/3.
u1-L5-Q7.2: 2h/3 is the distance from the apex, not from the base.
u1-L5-Q7.3: h/4 is not the centroid height for any standard shape.

u1-L5-Q8.0: bh^3/6 is the section modulus S = I/c, not the moment of inertia about the base.
u1-L5-Q8.1: bh^3/12 is the centroidal moment of inertia; the base axis requires the parallel axis theorem addition.
u1-L5-Q8.2: bh^3/4 does not correspond to any standard moment of inertia formula for a rectangle.

u1-L5-Q9.0: The moment of inertia integrates y^2 * dA, and since y^2 is always non-negative, I is always positive.

u1-L5-Q10.0: 150 mm assumes the centroid is at the flange center, ignoring the web contribution.
u1-L5-Q10.1: 100 mm is the geometric midpoint, but the wider flange shifts the centroid upward.
u1-L5-Q10.3: 120 mm does not match the correct weighted-average calculation for this T-section geometry.

u1-L5-Q11.0: The parallel axis theorem works for any shape; it is not restricted to rectangular sections.
u1-L5-Q11.1: Transfer distance direction matters; you must always transfer from the centroidal axis, not between arbitrary axes.
u1-L5-Q11.2: Subtracting Ad^2 transfers inward to the centroid; the standard theorem adds Ad^2 to transfer outward.

u1-L5-Q12.0: pi*d^4/12 is not a standard formula for any common geometric property of a circle.
u1-L5-Q12.1: pi*d^4/32 is the polar moment of inertia divided by 2, or the section modulus for torsion, not the area moment of inertia.
u1-L5-Q12.2: pi*d^2/4 is the area of a circle, not the moment of inertia.

u1-L5-Q13.1: Treating the hole as a negative area in the weighted centroid calculation is the standard and correct approach.

u1-L5-Q14.0: Adding d^4 instead of subtracting it would increase I, but the hole removes material and must be subtracted.
u1-L5-Q14.2: pi(D^2-d^2)/4 is the cross-sectional area of the tube, not its moment of inertia.
u1-L5-Q14.3: pi(D^4-d^4)/32 is the polar moment of inertia J, not the area moment of inertia I.

u1-L5-Q15.0: An off-center centroid affects bending stress distribution, which is critical for bending analysis, not just torsion.
u1-L5-Q15.1: Single C-channels are commonly used alone, and the asymmetric centroid must be accounted for.
u1-L5-Q15.3: C-channels are asymmetric sections; their centroids are not at the geometric center.

u1-L5-Q17.0: 37,500 mm is I/A directly, not the radius of gyration which requires the square root.
u1-L5-Q17.2: 5.16 mm is far too small; it likely results from an incorrect unit conversion.
u1-L5-Q17.3: 612 mm is much larger than the correct value and does not match sqrt(I/A).

u1-L5-Q18.1: L-shapes are easily decomposed into rectangles; formal integration is unnecessary.
u1-L5-Q18.2: The centroid location depends on the dimensions of the L and is not always at the inner corner.
u1-L5-Q18.3: An L-shape is not a half-rectangle or a triangle; the triangle centroid formula does not apply.

u1-L5-Q19.1: The perpendicular axis theorem J = Ix + Iy is valid for all planar shapes, relating polar and area moments of inertia.

u1-L5-Q20.0: a^4/6 is the section modulus S = I/c, not the moment of inertia.
u1-L5-Q20.1: a^4/4 does not correspond to any standard formula for a square cross-section.
u1-L5-Q20.3: a^4/3 is the moment of inertia about the edge (using parallel axis theorem), not about the centroid.

u1-L5-Q21.0: 80% incorrectly assumes J scales linearly with the diameter ratio.
u1-L5-Q21.2: 20% vastly underestimates J retention; the hollow shaft retains most of the outer material where J contribution is greatest.
u1-L5-Q21.3: 96% would imply the inner core contributes almost nothing, but a 0.8D hole removes about 41% of J.

u1-L5-Q22.0: R/3 is the centroid height for a triangle, not a semicircle.
u1-L5-Q22.1: R/2 is a common incorrect guess; the exact value 4R/(3pi) is less than R/2.
u1-L5-Q22.2: 2R/pi is the centroid of a half-ring (semicircular arc), not a filled semicircular area.

u1-L5-Q24.0: Ixy relates to bending-axis coupling, not torsional stiffness.
u1-L5-Q24.1: Ixy is important for unsymmetric bending and principal axis determination.
u1-L5-Q24.2: Ixy vanishes for any axis of symmetry, not just for circular cross-sections.

u1-L5-Q25.1: I = bh^3/12, so doubling h gives 8 times the original I, confirming the eightfold increase.

u1-L5-Q26.1: No gap correction factor is needed; the x-axes are coincident for back-to-back channels.
u1-L5-Q26.2: Both channels share the same centroidal x-axis, so no parallel axis transfer is needed for Ix.
u1-L5-Q26.3: The total Ix is simply the sum of individual Ix values when centroidal axes coincide.

u1-L5-Q27.0: Deflection depends on the full moment of inertia I and span, not the section modulus.
u1-L5-Q27.2: Shear force is determined from the loading, not from the section modulus.
u1-L5-Q27.3: Radius of gyration is sqrt(I/A), which does not involve the extreme fiber distance c.

u1-L5-Q28.0: Symmetry may eliminate the transfer in one direction but the composite centroid must still be found first.
u1-L5-Q28.1: Polar moments are calculated after, not before, finding the composite centroid and applying the parallel axis theorem.
u1-L5-Q28.2: Individual centroidal I values are inputs to the parallel axis theorem, found before but used after finding the composite centroid.

u1-L5-Q29.1: Distributing material far from the neutral axis, as a hollow tube does, increases I more effectively than a solid square.
